### Post by Barmaleychik on 2009-04-26
I am trying to get 1600x1200 resolution in 9.04 fresh install. The highest I can get in system/preferences/display is 1280x1024

I red in the forums that I need to edit my xorg.conf file. I found it in Krusader and look it up in F3 viewer of Krusader. It looks like this:


# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# Note that some configuration settings that could be done previously
# in this file, now are automatically configured by the server and settings
# here are ignored.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

********************* end of xorg.conf ***********************

I expected some numbers etc. Do I have a corrupted xorg.conf? How do I fix it?  Where do I go from here in solving my screen resolution problem?

Thank you in advance

---

### Post by Barmaleychik on 2009-04-26
Just to add to previous post: I use dvi video connector on Samsung SincMaster 204T. Can DVI be a reason my xorg.conf file was not configured right?

### Post by kerry_s on 2009-04-26
nope that's right it's usually empty.

```

Section "Screen"
Identifier "Default Screen"
Monitor "Configured Monitor"
Device "Configured Video Device"
[B]	DefaultDepth 24 
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth     24
		Modes  "1600x1200"
	EndSubSection[/B]
EndSection


```

---

### Post by Barmaleychik on 2009-04-26
Kerry S

Thank you very much for answering. Can I using notepad change lines in my xorg.conf to the lines you supplied safely to get 1600x1200 resolution. Are there any thing I need to be careful about in doing so?

Thank you again,

Barmaleychik

---

### Post by kerry_s on 2009-04-26
press> **alt+f2**
type> **gksudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf**

copy and paste that part in to xorg.conf.
if you type it watch the spelling, it's important, a lower case where a upper case and vice versa, will make it fail.

---

#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated

### Post by dagrump on 2009-04-26
Well, in your other post you mention a nvidia card, have you installed the drivers yet?
It's possible that that might be the issue. Look at system>admin>hardware drivers & see if it recognizes the video card. If it does it will download & install them.

---

### Post by Barmaleychik on 2009-04-26
Dagramp,

Thank you for your advice. I already thought about it and was trying to activate third party drivers. Hardware monitor found 3 nvidia drivers and offered to activate one of them of my choice. Initially I got an error message (I do not remember what it was exactly); now I keep getting a window which says that downloading drivers but after a minute window goes and nothing happens. Any ideas?

---

### Post by dagrump on 2009-04-26
You could use the package manager to install the recommended drivers, you want to make sure you use the recommended one. It's system>admin>synaptic. You need to hit reload & allow the machine to sync with the repositories then scroll down to find the package, should be something like nvidia-180-glx, mark the one recommend for your card & it will pop up a window with other needed files, mark those & hit apply.
See where that gets you. My machines require more tinkering, but it's normally not needed with single card setups.

---

### Post by sgosnell on 2009-04-26
Did you reboot?  Changes to xorg.conf won't take effect until you reboot, or at least restart X.

---

Then this:

Using X configuration file: "/etc/X11/xorg.conf".

ERROR: Unable to write to directory '/etc/X11'.

### Post by dagrump on 2009-04-27
First let's try what it says to do, application>accessorys>terminal, type in sudo nvidia-xconfig. it will ask for your password, after that it should give a warning about overwriting a customized xorg.conf, this is because you edited it earlier.
Now type exit, & reboot. We should be there.

---

### Post by Kareeser on 2009-04-27
Have you restarted the X server after running nvidia-xconfig? (Logout and Log back in)

After that, try running "gksudo nvidia-settings" to set up your monitor. This is the tool that comes with nVIDIA.

---

### Post by Barmaleychik on 2009-04-27
When I run gksudo nvidia-settings I am getting the message:

You do not appear to be using the NVIDIA X driver. Please edit your X configuration file (just run `nvidia-xconfig` as root), and restart the X server. 

What line should I add to X conf file?

---

### Post by sgosnell on 2009-04-27
Do what it says.  Run nvidia-xconfig as root, and it will fix the configuration files for you.  In a terminal, type "sudo nvidia-xconfig", and configure as you want.

---

### Post by Barmaleychik on 2009-04-27
Thank you guys, I got 1600x1200 resolution, so problem solved!

I am not sure why it works but it does! I re-booted again and it is working now( i did re-boot before many times but it did not help).

I guess the solution was in nvidia drivers been installed. Now i have everything graphics related recognized, including my monitor!

Thanks again!

---
